input.initialPrompt: A developer reports that CLI builds with 'dotnet build' are slow for incremental builds, even when nothing has changed. Visual Studio builds the same project almost instantly. Review the project and developer report, then suggest how to improve CLI build performance for the dev inner loop.

data.reasoningText: Let me review the project files and developer report to understand the issue.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. ## Developer Complaint
2. 
3. Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
4. for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
5. while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
6. 
7. We've already verified:
8. - No analyzers are slowing things down
9. - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
10. - The project itself compiles in <500ms
11. 
12. The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
13. project graph on every CLI invocation.
14. 
15. We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
16. command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.
17.

 We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the

data.content:
Here's my analysis:

## Root Cause

The developer report nails it: the overhead is **MSBuild process startup + project re-evaluation**, not compilation. Visual Studio avoids this because it keeps MSBuild nodes and the Roslyn compiler server warm between builds. The CLI (`dotnet build`) tears them down after every invocation by default.

## Recommendations (highest impact first)

### 1. Enable the MSBuild Server (biggest win)
Set this environment variable in your shell profile:
```
export DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1
```
This keeps an MSBuild server process alive between builds, eliminating startup/evaluation overhead — the same mechanism VS uses.

### 2. Enable Shared Roslyn Compilation
Add `<UseSharedCompilation>` to the project so the Roslyn compiler server (`VBCSCompiler`) stays resident:

arguments.new_str:
<PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <!-- Keep the Roslyn compiler server alive between builds (matches VS behavior) -->
    <UseSharedCompilation>true</UseSharedCompilation>
  </PropertyGroup>

data.reasoningText:
The developer reports that `dotnet build` from CLI is slow for incremental builds (2-4 seconds even when nothing changed), while Visual Studio builds nearly instantly. They've ruled out analyzers and target skipping issues. The overhead is from MSBuild process startup and project graph re-evaluation.

The key insight here is that Visual Studio keeps an MSBuild server process alive (warm), so it doesn't pay the startup cost each time. The CLI `dotnet build` starts a fresh MSBuild process each invocation.

There are several recommendations I can make:

1. **Use MSBuild Server (`-nodeReuse:true` / MSBuild Server)**: Starting with .NET 8, MSBuild has a "server" mode. By default, `dotnet build` uses `-nodeReuse:false`, meaning worker nodes are killed after each build. 

Visual Studio keeps nodes alive between builds. I can enable this by setting `MSBUILDDISABLENODEREUSE=0` or `DOTNET_CLI_DO_NOT_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=0`, or through the project property for shared compilation. In .NET 8+, there's also `DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1` which maintains an MSBuild server process between invocations to eliminate repeated startup costs.

The core issue is that `dotnet build` disables node reuse by default, tearing down MSBuild worker nodes after each build, whereas Visual Studio keeps them alive. I can fix this by setting `MSBUILDDISABLENODEREUSE` to `0` (which enables reuse) or passing `-nodeReuse:true` to the build command.

Beyond that, there are several other performance levers: enabling the Roslyn shared compilation server with `<UseSharedCompilation>true</UseSharedCompilation>` in the project file avoids re-launching the compiler for each build, using `dotnet build -graph` constructs a static build graph for faster evaluation, skipping restore with `--no-restore` when packages haven't changed, and disabling the terminal logger with `-tl:off` in .NET 8+ if the overhead isn't needed.

The most impactful change for the project would be adding that shared compilation setting to enable the Roslyn compiler server.

    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <!-- Keep the Roslyn compiler server alive between builds (matches VS behavior) -->
    <UseSharedCompilation>true</UseSharedCompilation>
  </PropertyGroup>

data.content:
### 3. Skip restore on repeat builds
When packages haven't changed, use:
```
dotnet build --no-restore
```
This avoids NuGet restore evaluation on every build.

### 4. Consider `dotnet watch` for edit-build-test loops
```
dotnet watch run
```
This keeps the host process alive and does file-watching-triggered incremental rebuilds — the closest CLI equivalent to the VS inner loop.

## Summary

| Technique | Saves | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| `DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1` | MSBuild startup + eval (~1-2s) | env var (all projects) |
| `UseSharedCompilation` | Roslyn compiler startup (~0.5-1s) | per-project (added above) |
| `--no-restore` | NuGet restore check (~0.5s) | per-invocation |
| `dotnet watch` | All of the above | workflow change |

The combination of the MSBuild server + shared compilation should bring CLI incremental builds close to VS-level speed.
